title: Trees Glow During Storms! Scientists Finally Prove It
summary:
Imagine standing under a tree during a thunderstorm—and seeing it glow! That might sound like a scene from a fantasy movie, but scientists just proved it's real.

For more than 70 years, researchers suspected that trees might give off tiny electrical sparks during storms. But nobody had ever caught it happening in nature. Until now.

A team from Penn State University packed a special van with a telescope on the roof and drove to North Carolina. They pointed their instrument at a sweetgum tree during a huge thunderstorm. And guess what? They saw it: 859 tiny bursts of electricity called "corona discharges" shooting from the leaves!

These sparks are too faint for your eyes to see, but they glow in ultraviolet (UV) light—like a blacklight poster. Each spark lasts only a fraction of a second.

Here's the cool part: these sparks actually help clean the air! They create a chemical called hydroxyl, which breaks down pollution and even greenhouse gases like methane.

"This just goes to show that there's still discovery science being done," said Patrick McFarland, the lead scientist. "For more than half a century, scientists have theorized that corona exists, but this proves it."

So next time you're in a storm, remember: the trees might be putting on a secret light show—and cleaning the air while they're at it!
why_it_matters: Trees are already amazing for giving us oxygen, and now you know they secretly help clean the air during storms—so taking care of trees helps take care of the planet.

background_read: Lightning during thunderstorms is a giant spark of electricity in the sky.
Trees can sometimes give off smaller sparks from their leaves when the electric field in the air is strong.
These tiny sparks, called corona discharges, have been a theory for decades but were never seen in nature until now.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Penn State University
WHAT: They proved trees glow with tiny sparks called corona discharges during storms
WHY: To prove a 70-year-old theory and show the sparks help clean the air

perspectives.description: This is a big moment for science—it proves a long-held theory and shows nature is even more incredible than we knew.
The fact that the sparks create hydroxyl, which cleans pollution, could help scientists find new ways to clean the air.
The team only studied a few trees in one storm; we need more research to see if all trees do this and how much air cleaning happens.
